examples.title: Singly Linked List - Node Creation and Traversal
examples.description: C program to create a linked list with 3 nodes and traverse to display elements
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates basic linked list operations in C. The struct Node defines the node with data and next pointer. createNode() allocates memory using malloc and initializes the node. display() traverses the list starting from head, printing each node's data until it reaches NULL.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for traversal, O(1) for node creation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes

examples.title: Insertion at Beginning in Linked List
examples.description: Python class implementation for inserting a node at the beginning of linked list
examples.code:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, data):
        self.data = data
        self.next = None

class LinkedList:
    def __init__(self):
        self.head = None
    
    def insert_at_beginning(self, data):
        # Create new node
        new_node = Node(data)
        # Point new node to current head
        new_node.next = self.head
        # Update head to new node
        self.head = new_node
    
    def display(self):
        temp = self.head
        while temp:
            print(temp.data, end=" -> ")
            temp = temp.next
        print("None")

# Usage
ll = LinkedList()
ll.insert_at_beginning(30)
ll.insert_at_beginning(20)
ll.insert_at_beginning(10)
ll.display()  # Output: 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> None
examples.explanation: The insert_at_beginning method creates a new node with given data, makes it point to the current head (which could be None for empty list), then updates the head to point to the new node. This is O(1) operation. Python's None is equivalent to NULL in C.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for insertion, O(n) for storing n nodes

examples.title: Deletion from Beginning in Linked List
examples.description: Java implementation for deleting the first node from linked list
examples.explanation: The deleteFirst() method checks if list is empty, stores the current head in a temporary variable, moves head pointer to the next node, and the old head is automatically garbage collected. In Java, we don't need explicit free() like in C. Time complexity is O(1).

examples.title: Reversing a Linked List
examples.description: Algorithm to reverse a singly linked list in-place using three pointers
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ReverseLinkedList(head)
BEGIN
    PREV ← NULL
    CURRENT ← head
    NEXT ← NULL
    
    WHILE CURRENT ≠ NULL DO
        // Store next node
        NEXT ← CURRENT.next
        
        // Reverse the pointer
        CURRENT.next ← PREV
        
        // Move pointers forward
        PREV ← CURRENT
        CURRENT ← NEXT
    END WHILE
    
    // Update head to last node
    head ← PREV
    RETURN head
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm reverses the linked list in-place without allocating extra memory. We use three pointers: PREV (tracks previous node), CURRENT (node being processed), and NEXT (stores next node before we overwrite CURRENT.next). After the loop, PREV points to the new head (originally last node). This is a fundamental algorithm in linked list operations.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - we visit each node exactly once
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - only three pointers used regardless of list size
